key_points: The Sultanate faced a 'Crisis of Legitimacy' as Tawarikh authors emphasized 'Birthright' and 'Gender Distinctions,' which often clashed with the Sultans' practical need to appoint merit-based 'Bandagan' (special slaves).

The 'Internal Frontier' aimed at consolidation through the deforestation of the Ganga-Yamuna doab, while the 'External Frontier' involved deep-strike military expeditions into South India under Malik Kafur.
Alauddin Khalji’s administration was 'Defensive-Bureaucratic,' focusing on price controls and market regulations to sustain a large standing army against Mongol incursions.

Muhammad Tughluq’s administration was 'Offensive-Experimental,' characterized by the failed 'Token Currency' system, the disastrous shift of the capital to Daulatabad, and an abortive plan to invade Transoxiana.

The 15th and 16th centuries saw the emergence of 'Sher Shah Suri,' whose administrative reforms in land revenue and communication were so effective they later served as a model for the Mughal Emperor Akbar.
examples: Comparative Defence: Alauddin Khalji built the garrison town of 'Siri' for his soldiers, whereas Muhammad Tughluq emptied the residents of 'Dehli-i-Kuhna' to house his army.
Fiscal Policy: Alauddin collected 50% yield as 'Kharaj' and strictly monitored market prices; Tughluq increased taxes in the Doab during a famine, leading to widespread peasant rebellion.
The Ibn Battuta Records: A 14th-century Moroccan traveler whose accounts provide a critical 'outsider' perspective on the Sultanate’s postal system and social life.
Sayyid and Lodi Dynasties: Represented a shift toward a more decentralized Afghan model of kingship before the final Mughal conquest in 1526.
formulas: State Stability = $\text{Loyalty of Muqtis} + \text{Prosperity of Peasants (Circle of Justice)} - \text{Mongol Invasions}$
exam_tips: Critically analyze the 'Circle of Justice'—it proves that the Sultans understood that military power was entirely dependent on peasant welfare.
Understand that the 'Bandagan' were more than slaves; they were a loyal elite used to bypass the power of troublesome, high-born nobles.
Note the failure of the 'Token Currency': It failed because people began minting their own coins at home using cheap metals like copper and brass.
Differentiate between the 'Garrison Town' (military core) and 'Hinterland' (the economic backyard that feeds the core).
